landing.title: RFC 1149
landing.subtitle: A Standard for the Transmission of IP Datagrams on Avian Carriers
landing.description: In 1990, a joke became a standard. In 2001, that standard beat ADSL. This is its reference implementation.

docs.title: The RFC Family
docs.subtitle: Three decades of avian infrastructure standards

rfc1149.title: A Standard for the Transmission of IP Datagrams on Avian Carriers
rfc1149.summary: The foundational document. Defines IP transmission over homing pigeons, including segment size (256 milligrams), maximum transmission unit, and error detection via examining the carrier.

rfc2549.title: IP over Avian Carriers with Quality of Service
rfc2549.summary: Extends RFC 1149 with QoS markings. Defines differentiated services for avian transport: priority traffic may be carried by faster birds (swifts); low-priority traffic by slower species.

bergen.title: Real-world implementation
bergen.summary: The Bergen Linux User Group implemented RFC 1149 in practice. Nine packets were sent. Ping response time: 3,000–6,000 seconds. Packet loss: 55%. The surviving pigeons beat the local ADSL connection in effective throughput.

rfc6214.title: Adaptation of RFC 1149 for IPv6
rfc6214.summary: Updates RFC 1149 for IPv6. Notes that the increased address space (128 bits) requires larger leg bands. Recommends using longer-lived bird species for addresses with low TTL.

doac.title: DNS over Avian Carriers
doac.summary: The missing piece: how does a host on an avian-carrier network resolve a hostname? Defines the AA resource record, the Pigeon of Last Resort (PoLR), and retransmission behavior. Threat model includes Hawk-in-the-Middle (HitM) attacks and the Hungry Cat as a physical layer threat.

notFound.title: NXPIGEON
notFound.description: The carrier arrived at the destination loft, but no datagram was found. The requested resource does not exist or was never transmitted.
